segments.s171-g57: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. گودرز and کشواد are two proper names in sequence (subject/agents); Persian often strings names without a conjunction to list actors. بر is the preposition 'toward/to' governing the following noun میسره, so the phrase means 'advanced toward/against Mīsera' (location or target).
segments.s171-g58: هجیر (a proper name) is joined by و 'and' to گرانمایگان, here 'the illustrious/important men' (an epithet for nobles/warriors). یکسره is an adverb modifying the whole phrase meaning 'altogether/straightaway' (they acted as one or immediately). The segment completes the temporal clause begun by چو: when A and B moved, Hijir and the nobles all at once (did X).
segments.s171-g59: به ... اندرون marks direction or target: 'into/to the inner part of'. قلب 'heart' is the direct object and اندرون 'inside/inner' qualifies it; رستم زابلی is a proper-name apposition 'Rostam, the Zābeli' specifying whose heart is meant. Word order is typical Persian 'to/into + noun + modifier + name' for a directional phrase.
segments.s171-g60: زره‌دار is an agent-noun 'armored one/armored warrior' (here the attacker). با introduces the instrument/companion 'with', followed by خنجر 'dagger' and کابلی 'Kabul‑born' (an adjective/nisba). The noun phrase is telegraphic (no main verb) and functions appositionally to the previous directional phrase: 'to the inner heart of Rostam — an armored man with a Kabul dagger (did it)'.
segments.s171-g61: تو گفتی here functions as a verbal phrase leading into the observation (in older/poetic register it can introduce a reported or dramatic statement — 'it was said/so it seemed'). نه شب بود پیدا نه روز is a balanced, negative coordination: 'neither night (was) present/visible nor (was there) day' — بود is the past copula 'was' and پیدا qualifies as 'visible/apparent'. Persian often omits repeated verbs after the first, so بود applies to both شب and روز.
segments.s171-g62: نهان گشت is a compound: نهان 'hidden' and گشت (past/compound of گشتن) 'became/turned'. خورشید 'the sun' is modified by the epithet گیتی‌فروز 'world‑illuminating' (a compound adjective). The clause states that the sun turned hidden — i.e., the world‑lighting sun was concealed.
segments.s171-g63: شد 'became' is the main verb. از introduces the cause/source 'from'; سم اسپان is a genitive: سم 'hoof' governed by اسپان 'of the horses' (old plural of اسپ/اسب). زمین 'the ground' is the subject/scene and سنگ رنگ 'stone‑colored' is a compound adjective 'of stone color' (literal 'stone color'). Word order is verb–source–subject–predicate, common in poetic compression.
segments.s171-g64: ز (poetic form of از) again marks origin 'from'; نیزه 'spear' is the source, and هوا 'the air/atmosphere' is the affected medium. همچو 'like' introduces a simile; پشت 'back' and پلنگ 'leopard' complete it: 'the air from the spears was like the back of a leopard' — a vivid simile describing spears' tips or patterns in the air.
segments.s171-g33: خروش آمد is a simple nominal phrase (noun + come) meaning “a roar arose” — خروش (noun) is the subject and آمد (past of آمدن) is the verb 'came/rose'. The ו here links this clause to what follows: ناله کرنای is a compact noun‑noun phrase (nâleh + karnâ) where ناله 'lament/cry' is understood as the sound produced by کرنای 'the trumpet' (poetic apposition instead of an explicit ezâfe).
segments.s171-g34: دم نای is another noun+noun sequence: دم 'blast/breath' specifies the kind of sound of نای 'reed‑pipe/flute'. نای is followed by the adjectives رویین و هندی (iron‑/metal and Indian); درای is a poetic form related to دارای (having/possessing) used postpositively to indicate those qualities — so the phrase describes the blasts of pipes that are iron (رویین) and Indian (هندی). Word order is typical poetic Persian (noun + modifier; postpositive adjective/participle).
segments.s171-g35: زمین آمد ... به جوش is an idiomatic verbal phrase: آمد به جوش 'came to a boil/surge' — زمین (the earth) is the subject. از سم اسپان gives the cause/source: از 'from' + سم 'the hoof' (here the hoofs of horses) and اسپان is the plural 'horses' (so 'from the horses' hooves'). The prepositional phrase indicates what made the earth 'boil' (i.e. the pounding of many hooves).
segments.s171-g36: به ابر اندر آمد is a circumstantial prepositional phrase: به ... اندر آمد literally 'into the cloud came' (note the double directionals به + اندر for poetic emphasis). آمد is the verb 'came' and governs the coordinated nouns فغان و خروش 'cries and roars' (فغان = lament/cry, خروش = roar). The order places the place/medium ('into the cloud') before the coming of the sounds.
segments.s171-g37: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برخاست (past of برخاستن) means 'rose' or 'got up'. از دشت indicates origin 'from the plain'. گرد سپاه is a noun phrase where گرد means 'dust' or 'cloud' (here the dust-cloud kicked up by the army) and سپاه 'army' specifies whose dust-cloud it is; thus the segment describes the army rising from the plain in a dust‑cloud.
segments.s171-g38: کس آمد is an indefinite subject 'someone came'. بر رستم here uses بر with the sense 'toward/before' (came before Rostam). از دیده‌گاه is a locative phrase 'from the field of sight' = 'into (his) view'; Persian often uses از + دیده‌گاه to express 'into sight'. The whole segment means 'someone came into Rostam's sight.'
segments.s171-g39: که at the start of the clause introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'). آمد سپاهی is the verb phrase 'an army came'. چو کوه گران is a simile: چو 'like/as' + کوه 'mountain' + گران 'heavy/weighty' — 'an army like a weighty mountain.' Word order places the simile after the noun for emphasis, typical of epic diction.
segments.s171-g40: همه رزم جویان is a compound noun phrase 'all the battle‑seekers' (= all the warriors). کندآوران is an agentive/participial formation meaning 'those who make (others) slow/checked' (poetic formation: کند + آوران → 'causers of slowing'). Together the phrase says that the arriving host caused all the warriors to be checked/halted — the verbal adjective functions predicatively about the effect of that host.
segments.s171-g25: بزد is a past/epic simple verb 'he struck' (subject omitted but clear from context). نای رویین is a compound noun phrase: نای 'ney, reed-flute' modified by رویین 'iron/hard', so 'the iron ney'. The conjunction و links the two actions; بربست here is a poetic/archaic verb meaning he made the drum sound/struck the drum (literally 'bound/fastened' in its lexical senses, but used idiomatically for sounding the drum). کوس is the object 'war-drum'.
segments.s171-g26: همی is an adverb of continuity 'continually/always' placed before the subject phrase. آسمان بر زمین is a prepositional/locative phrase 'the sky upon the earth' (بر = 'upon'). داد is the past of دادن 'gave', and بوس is the noun 'kiss' used here with داد to mean 'gave a kiss' (a poetic image for a loud meeting of sky and earth).
segments.s171-g27: به marks the indirect object 'to/at' and is followed by گردنکشان 'the neck-stretchers/rebels' (a pejorative name). خسرو is the subject (positioned after the object in poetic inversion). آواز کرد is a light-verb construction 'made a voice / called out' (کرد here = 'did/made').
segments.s171-g28: که‌ای is a vocative interjection ('that/you who' + the vocative ای), addressing the addressees directly as 'O ...'. نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'renowned ones'. روز نبرد is a genitival phrase 'the day of battle' (روز = 'day'; نبرد = 'battle'), specifying which fame is meant.
segments.s171-g29: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. برخیزد is a present/subjunctive-third-person form 'rises/arises' (used after چو to indicate the event). آوای کوس is the noun phrase 'the sound/voice of the drum' and از دو روی means 'from both sides' (روی here = 'side/surface').
segments.s171-g30: نجوید is a negative present form: the initial ن- is the negative prefix and جوید (from جستن/جُویدن) would be 'seeks'; thus نجوید = 'does not seek/does not favor'. زمان is the subject 'time'. مرد پرخاشجوی is the object/predicate phrase 'an aggressive/quarrelsome man' (مرد here = man/person; پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive').
segments.s171-g31: همه رزم را places 'all the battle/warriors' as a definite object (را marks specificity) — effectively 'all the warriors'. دل پر از کین کنید is an imperative/optative construction: دل 'the heart(s)' + پر از 'full of' + کین 'hatred/vengeance', with کنید (2nd-person plural) 'make/do' giving 'make the hearts full of hatred'.
segments.s171-g32: به introduces the target of the curse 'upon/to the Iranians'. ایرانیان پاک is 'the pure Iranians' (پاک modifying ایرانیان). نفرین is the noun 'curse' and کنید is the same 2nd-person-plural imperative 'do/make' — together 'curse the pure Iranians.'
segments.s171-g9: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase meaning 'the whole city/people of Iran' (همه before the noun = 'all/every'). جگر خسته‌اند is the predicate: جگر here is metaphorical 'heart/seat of feeling' and خسته‌اند uses the plural suffix -اند (they are tired/grief‑stricken); the subject is the previously mentioned 'all (people of) Iran' though grammatically the phrase is singular-looking.
segments.s171-g10: به کین marks purpose/object 'for/by/with the vengeance (of)', followed by سیاوش as the person whose vengeance is meant. کمر بسته‌اند is an idiom 'have girded the loins / prepared (for action)'; بسته‌اند is perfective/resultative with 3rd-person plural -اند agreeing with the same collective subject as before.
segments.s171-g11: نگون شد is a predicative phrase meaning 'was overthrown / came low' (نگون here = overthrown/downfallen) with شد as the copular/auxiliary 'became/was'. سر و تاج افراسیاب is the compound nominal (head and crown) belonging to Afrasiab; order places the predicate before the noun phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s171-g12: همی کند is the epic/poetic progressive (همی + present-form کند) meaning 'he was doing/kept doing'. موی is the direct object 'hair' (he plucks/tears at his hair). و همی ریخت آب uses همی + ریخت ('was pouring/shedding') where ریخت here denotes 'pouring/flowing' (tears), and آب is 'water' (literal or figurative tears).
segments.s171-g13: همی گفت is the epic present 'he said' (همی marking habitual/narrative present). The following words are vocatives/appositions: رادا (here a proper name) followed by سرا موبدا — 'house (سرا) mobed (موبدا)' where the linking ezāfe is omitted for poetic compression, so these three items are addressed together.
segments.s171-g14: This sequence lists the object/appeal: ردا here (different word from the proper name above) is the noun 'rida/robe' and نامدارا is its adjective 'renowned/famed' (adjective follows the noun in this line). یلا is a vocative proper name 'Yela', and بخردا is an archaic/formal vocative/address meaning 'O wise one' (the form functions as an address to the wise person). The verb 'bring' or similar is implied rather than explicit.
segments.s171-g15: دریغ at the start is an exclamatory particle 'alas / what a pity'. ارغوانی رخت is the noun phrase 'purple robe' (color adjective before noun in this line), and همچو ماه is a simile 'like the moon' (همچو = 'like/as'). The whole is an exclamatory lament about the beautiful purple garment.
segments.s171-g16: Again دریغ is 'alas'. آن is the demonstrative/determiner 'that/the', followed by کیی (a Kayanian/title) referring to the person lamented. برز and بالای function as adjectival/epithetical words 'exalted / above', and شاه names the rank 'king' — together 'that Kayi, exalted and superior among kings.' Note poetic compression: ezāfe links are omitted.
segments.s171-g41: 'ز تیغ دلیران' is a prepositional/causal phrase ('from/by the swords of the brave') followed by the clause 'هوا شد بنفش' where شد is the past of شدن 'to become' and بنفش is a predicate adjective. Word order is poetic: possessor 'دلیران' follows تیغ, and the whole prepositional phrase licenses the resulting state of the air.
segments.s171-g42: برفتند is simple past 'they went/advanced'; the prepositional phrase 'با کاویانی درفش' follows and indicates accompaniment/means ('with the Kayanian banner'). 'کاویانی' qualifies درفش (the Kayanian standard). The construction is straightforward past narrative.
segments.s171-g43: برآمد خروش سپاه is 'a cry/roar of the army arose' — برآمد (arose/issued forth) is the verb, خروش (roar/cry) is the subject and سپاه (army) is in a genitive relationship (the army's cry). 'از دو روی' is an adverbial phrase 'from two sides' using روی in the sense 'side'.
segments.s171-g44: جهان شد پر از مردم جنگجوی uses شد 'became' with the predicate 'پر از' ('full of') plus the noun phrase 'مردم جنگجوی' ('people/folk who are warriors'). The adjective/compound جنگجوی modifies مردم; the whole means the world was filled with warriors.
segments.s171-g45: خور و ماه is a coordinated subject 'the sun and the moon.' گفتی here functions as an impersonal/poetic 'one says' or 'it is said / you might say.' 'به رنگ اندرست' is a stative clause: به رنگ = 'in/with color', and اندرست (archaic) = 'is within / is among,' so the clause says the sun and moon are 'in color' (i.e. brightly colored/visible).
segments.s171-g46: ستاره (star) is the subject and 'به چنگ نهنگ اندرست' is a locative-stative predicate: به + noun + اندرست = 'is in/within' the noun. چنگ here means 'claw/grip' (not the musical instrument) and نهنگ is 'whale,' so the image is 'the star is in the whale's claw.'
segments.s171-g47: سپهدار ترکان is a genitive phrase 'the commander of the Turks.' برآراست is a past/poetic form of آراستن with the prefix بر- meaning 'was arrayed / was made ready'; followed by جنگ ('for battle' / 'battle') indicating the purpose or situation — 'the Turks' commander was arrayed for war.'
segments.s171-g48: گرفتند is simple past 'they seized/took.' The direct objects are 'گوپال و خنجر' and the phrase 'به چنگ' is an adverbial 'into the hand / in hand' (چنگ here 'claw/hand, i.e. a grip'). Note: گوپال is ambiguous in this manuscript—it may be a proper name (Gopala) or a specific object; the syntax treats it as one coordinated object taken together with the dagger.
segments.s171-g17: خروشان is a participial/adverbial form meaning 'shrieking/roaring' and sets the manner for the action that follows. به ... سر ... بر ... پراگند خاک is the verbless clause that in context links to the main verb کرد later: literally 'scattered dust upon the head(s)'; word order is prepositional (به سر = onto the head) then the adjective/participle پراگند governing خاک (dust).
segments.s171-g18: همه جامه‌ها کرد چاک is a clause with جامه‌ها (direct object) preceding the verb کرد and the result/complement چاک following it: 'they made the garments torn' = 'they tore their garments.' بر خویش is a prepositional phrase 'upon themselves' (خوی + possessive ش). کرد is the past action verb ('did/made') serving to produce the state expressed by چاک.
segments.s171-g19: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the speech verb 'said' (here the listed senses do not include the verbal sense 'to say', so its sense is given as uncertain). با + لشکر افراسیاب is an accompaniment/instrumental phrase 'with his host' or 'to his army' identifying who is addressed or who accompanies the speaker; word order is inverted for poetic effect (the normal prose order would be افراسیاب با لشکر چنین گفت).
segments.s171-g20: که is the complementizer 'that'. مارا is the object/experiencer 'us'. بر آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'arose/came up' (بر as verbal prefix + آمد), and سر از خورد و خواب is the phrase giving what they were roused from: سر here literally 'head' used in the idiom 'سر بر آمد از X' meaning 'was roused/raised from X' — i.e. they were stirred from eating and sleep. Word order places مارا (us) before the verbal phrase.
segments.s171-g21: همه کینه را is the definite object 'every/ all hatred/grudge' marked by را. چشم روشن کنید is an imperative plural 'make your eyes bright / open your eyes' — روشن here is an adjective 'bright/clear' and چشم روشن کردن is idiomatic for becoming watchful/alert or directing one's gaze; کنید is the plural imperative 'do/make'. The object precedes the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s171-g22: نهالی ز خفتان ... کنید contains two imperatives joined: نهالی ... کنید is figurative — نهالی 'a sapling' implies 'sprout up' (i.e. rise up) and ز خفتان 'from sleep' gives the source; the second conjunct جوشن کنید is the imperative 'put on (your) cuirass/armor.' Overall this is an exhortation: 'spring up from sleep and put on armour.'
segments.s171-g23: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برخاست is the past verb 'rose/was raised.' آوای کوس is a genitive construction 'the sound/cry of the war-drum.' از درش 'from his gate' locates the source (در as 'gate/door' with the possessive suffix ش = 'his'). The clause is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو.
segments.s171-g24: بجنبید is a past/plural verb form meaning 'they stirred/moved' (here a narrative past: 'the army was roused'). بر بارگه لشکرش is a prepositional phrase 'at/onto the court (bargeh) his army' — لشکرش is 'his army' (lshkr + possessive). Word order places the verb before the locative phrase for poetic rhythm.
segments.s171-g49: بیامد is a third-person singular past verb 'came'. It is placed before its directional phrase (poetic/classical word order). سوی governs the following noun phrase 'میمنه بارمان' and means 'toward'; میمنه is 'the right (flank/side)' and بارمان is a proper name (Barman).
segments.s171-g50: سپاهی is a noun 'an army/host' (here a collective noun). The preposition ز (poetic for از) marks origin 'from', taking ترکان 'the Turks' as its object. دنان و دمان are presented appositively after ترکان (names of leaders/tribes); their specific lexical senses are not given in the provided list, so they function here as proper names. و is the coordinating 'and'.
segments.s171-g51: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. The following noun group 'میسره کهرم تیغ‌زن' is a compact noun phrase where میسره and کهرم appear as names/place or epithets and تیغ‌زن ('sword-striker') is an epithet/descriptor placed after them; تیغ‌زن is a compound noun meaning 'swordsman' or 'one who strikes with the blade'. The sequence shows the classical/poetic order of noun + epithet.
segments.s171-g52: به is the preposition here meaning 'into/towards' (motion into a place). قلب اندرون شاه is a genitive/possessive locative — 'the heart (inner part) of the interior of the king' (i.e., the king's inner chamber/presence). با انجمن is an instrumental/accompaniment phrase 'with (his) assembly/retinue'. Word order is preposition + locative phrase followed by the accompaniment.
segments.s171-g53: وزین روی is an adjectival phrase 'with a weighty/dignified face' (وزین modifies روی). رستم is the proper name (the subject/agent). سپه is the direct object 'the host/army' and برکشید is a past tense verb 'raised/lifted'. Classical poetry often places the qualifying phrase before the name and the verb after the object, so the clause reads 'Rostam, with that grave face, raised the army.'
segments.s171-g54: هوا شد is a light/impersonal construction 'the air/atmosphere became' or 'a commotion arose' (شد = became). The preposition ز marks cause 'because of / from', taking تیغ یلان 'the swords of the heroes/warriors' as the cause. ناپدید is a predicative adjective 'vanished/was made invisible', so the whole phrase means the air/atmosphere was obscured or vanished because of the warriors' swords.
segments.s171-g55: بیاراست is a past/transitive verb 'arrayed/arranged' (here used in epic style with the verb before its agents). بر میمنه is the locative 'on/upon the right (flank)'. گیو و طوس are the proper names that follow and function as agents/participants (Giv and Tus). In classical Persian the verb can precede the subject, so this reads 'Giv and Tus arrayed (themselves) on the right.'
segments.s171-g56: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' modified by the adjective بیدار 'alert/awake' which follows the noun (typical in this register). با پیل و کوس is an accompaniment/instrument phrase 'with elephants and drums' (با marking accompaniment or equipment). The phrase describes the composition and readiness of the mounted troops.
segments.s171-g137: همی + گشت is a literary progressive/persistent past: همی (an emphatic particle meaning 'continually/kept on') with گشت (here ‘go/roam’) gives 'kept circling/roaming'. با introduces the companion(s) ('with'), and آن دو یل پیلسم names those two fighters (پیلسم functions as a proper name or epithet). Word order is typical Persian (verb after particles, then prepositional phrase marking accompaniment).
segments.s171-g138: به میدان is a prepositional phrase 'into/toward the field' (میدان = battlefield/arena). به کردار is a fixed idiom 'in the manner/character of' (کردار = conduct, manner). شیر دژم is a descriptive simile: شیر 'lion' + دژم an archaic adjective 'grim/sullen', together 'like a grim lion.' The clauses string to describe how he moved in the field.
segments.s171-g139: بدانست here is a perfect/past 'he knew/realized' but in this line the subject that follows (تهمتن) is placed before the prepositional phrase for emphasis; ز قلب سپه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/out of the heart (center) of the host/army' (ز = from). بنگرید is the classical form of 'looked/gazed' (third-person past/preterite in Ferdowsī's diction).
segments.s171-g140: دو گرد functions as the object of دید 'he saw': 'two ...' followed by adjectives دلیر و گرانمایه 'brave and esteemed/noble.' گرد here denotes a unit/group or band of fighters (an archaic usage in epic diction) and is modified by the two adjectives; دید is simple past 'saw/beheld.'
segments.s171-g141: برآویخته is a past participle/poetic perfect meaning 'having leapt up' or 'sprung up' (here presenting a sudden action). با یکی شیرمرد attaches the companion/participant: 'with one champion' (یکی as 'a certain/some one'). The phrase connects who joins the sudden movement.
segments.s171-g142: به ابر اندر آورده is composed of به + ابر + اندر + آورده: 'brought into the cloud(s)/into the clouds' (آورده is the past participle 'brought'). از باد گرد is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/from a wind-whirl / dust-wind'—باد 'wind' and گرد here 'dust/whirl' (a compound poetic image indicating a gust or dust-storm that carries them up). The sequence describes the manner/instrument by which they were raised.
segments.s171-g143: بدانست is past 'he knew/realized'; the order 'بدانست رستم' is an inversion common in epic Persian (verb then subject). که introduces the content clause 'that ...', جز پیلسم means 'except Pil-sam' (جز = except/other than).
segments.s171-g144: ز ترکان = 'of/from the Turks' (poetic genitive). ندارد کس is a negative expression 'no one has' (ندارد = 'has/possesses' in present/past narrative tense; combined with کس here means 'there is no one who has'). آن زور و دم = 'that strength and vigor' (آن = 'that', زور = 'strength, might', دم = 'breath/vigor' used poetically for force). Together: 'no one among the Turks, except Pil-sam, has that strength and vigor.'
segments.s171-g65: تو گفتی is an address + 2nd-person past: تو = 'you' and گفتی = 'you said' (2nd singular past). هوا کوه آهن شدست is a compressed clause: هوا (agent/force) + کوه (object) + آهن شدست, where شدست is an archaic/poetic form equivalent to شده‌است ('has become'). Word order is topic/agent (هوا) before the patient (کوه) and the result (آهن شدست).
segments.s171-g66: سر کوه = 'the summit/top of the mountain' (noun + noun), then پر + [things] = 'full (of)'. Here پر functions as 'full (of)' followed by objects ترگ و جوشن. شدست again = 'has become/is' (archaic). The coordination و links ترگ (rags/shreds) and جوشن (armour/curiass).
segments.s171-g67: به ابر اندر آمد = 'into/toward the cloud entered' — به + اندر gives a strong locative/into sense. آمد is simple past 'came/entered'. سنان is used as the (collective) spear(s) and functions as the grammatical subject; Persian poetry often has a singular verb with a collective/aggregate subject. و درفش links the next item 'and banners'.
segments.s171-g68: درفشیدن here is a verbal/participial form describing the action of the banner (lit. 'banner-ing'); the exact lexical senses provided don't match classical usages, so this is an action-form related to banners (i.e., waving/brandishing). تیغهای بنفش = 'purple blades/swords' with تیغهای as plural (noun + plural suffix) and بنفش postpositively qualifying the noun (Persian places adjective after the noun in classical poetry).
segments.s171-g69: بیامد is past 'came' (here simple past). ز قلب سپه = 'from the heart/center of the host/army' (ز = from, قلب = heart/center, سپه = army). پیلسم is a proper-name or otherwise opaque word placed after the locative phrase as the emergent subject/figure. The sequence is source → place → person who arrived.
segments.s171-g70: دلش = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic possessive ش). پر ز خون کرده = a causative/resultative construction: پر = 'full (of)', ز خون = 'of blood', کرده = past participle/auxiliary 'made' or result 'had become' (so 'his heart was made/full of blood'). چهره دژم = 'a grim/sullen face' — verbless descriptive clause placing the appearance after the statement about his heart.
segments.s171-g71: چنین = 'thus/so' (adverb) modifying گفت = 'he said' (classical past). با شاه توران سپاه: با here marks the addressee/companionship; in classical usage گفت با X = 'said to X' or 'said in the presence of X', so the phrase names the Turanian king and the army as the audience/recipient of the speech.
segments.s171-g72: که‌ای is a poetic/archaic vocative combination (که + ای) used like 'O/you who are...' introducing a direct address. پرهنر = 'full/possessing skill' (an epithet), خسرو here is used as title/name 'Khosrow' (king), and نیک‌خواه is a compound adjective meaning 'well-wishing' or 'benevolent' — all three form a vocative phrase addressed to the king.
segments.s171-g249: برآشفت is a past tense (preterite) verb meaning ‘became angry’ or ‘flew into a rage’ and here is the main finite verb for this clause. گردافگن is an adjectival epithet (literally ‘dust-raising’) that modifies the following noun تاج‌بخش; the compound order is modifier + noun, a common poetic pattern. تاج‌بخش is an honorific noun for the king/sovereign (literally ‘crown-giver’) and functions as the subject of برآشفت.
segments.s171-g250: به دنبال is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning ‘in pursuit of’ or ‘after’; here به (used with دنبال) marks direction/toward. هومان is a proper name and the object of the pursuit. برانگیخت is a past tense verb ‘roused’ or ‘spurred’ and takes رخش as its object: the verb + object order is verb last (VSO/V-final is common in classical Persian narrative). رخش is the name of the steed (object) that is being roused.
segments.s171-g251: بتازید is an imperfect/past-form verb meaning ‘galloped/rushed’; in narrative it presents the action performed by the subject. چندی ... و ... چندی is an adverbial repetition (‘for a while and then for a while’) that emphasizes a period of time; the coordinating و links the two temporal phrases. شتافت is a past-tense verb ‘hastened/ran on’ and continues the sequence of movement — note the sequence of verbs describing successive actions.
segments.s171-g252: زمانه here means ‘fortune’ or fate (a common poetic sense), and بدش is composed of بد ‘bad/unfavourable’ plus the enclitic ش (‘his’), meaning ‘his bad fortune’. مانده is a past-participial/adjectival form ‘remained’ or ‘abided’ describing that misfortune clung to him. او را نیافت is a separate short clause: او (he) + را (definite object/accusative marker) + نیافت (‘did not find’); the negative past verb refers back to earlier pursuit — ‘he did not catch/find him.’
segments.s171-g253: سپهدار ‘commander’ is the subject, followed by ترکان ‘of the Turks’ in genitive relation (title + tribal name). نشد is the past-tense of ‘become’ and here negated by context to mean ‘did not become’ or ‘was not brought’ under control. زیر دست is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase meaning ‘under the hand’ i.e. ‘under one’s power/control’; the sequence ‘نشد زیر دست’ says he was not subdued.
segments.s171-g254: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘all at once’ or ‘suddenly’ (literally ‘one occasion’). تیزتگ is a noun meaning a small sharp blade/dagger (here the instrument that strikes). برنشست is a past verb literally ‘sat upon’ but used idiomatically of something settling or alighting on someone — in this martial context it means the sharp weapon ‘fell upon/struck’ him; the verb thus conveys an instantaneous, completed action.
segments.s171-g255: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning ‘when’. از جنگ رستم combines the preposition از ‘from’ with جنگ ‘battle’ and the name رستم, so the phrase means ‘from Rostam’s battle’ or ‘away from the fight with Rostam’. بپیچید is a past tense verb ‘turned’ (the prefix بـ plus verb is a common classical narrative form) and روی here means ‘face’ or ‘direction’ — together they express ‘turned his face away’ or ‘turned away’. The word order places the time/setting (چو از جنگ رستم) before the verb.
segments.s171-g256: گریزان is an adjective/participial form ‘fleeing’ used as a substantive descriptor for the man. همی is a classical presentive particle meaning ‘kept on / still’ and here marks the ongoing action. رفت is the past of ‘go’ (narrative past), indicating motion away. پرخاشجوی is an adjective meaning ‘aggressive’ or ‘quarrelsome’ that qualifies the subject; together the phrase describes someone who, though aggressive by nature, is fleeing and continues to go.
segments.s171-g1: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. لشکر is the subject 'army/host' and بیامد is the past tense verb 'came' (classical/poetic form of آمد). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and attaches to the following noun phrase دشت نبرد ('plain/battlefield'), so the whole segment reads 'When the army came from the battlefield.'
segments.s171-g2: This is a verbless descriptive clause: تنان (poetic plural of تن) 'bodies' and سران 'heads' are followed by the adjectival phrase پر ز + noun meaning 'full of ...'. پر is used here as 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', so تنان پر ز خون = 'bodies full of blood' and سران پر ز گرد = 'heads full of dust'. The coordinating و links the two parallel nominal phrases.
segments.s171-g3: خبر شد is an impersonal/predicative expression 'it was reported / news happened' (خبر 'news' + شد past). ز ترکان is the source 'from the Turks' and به افراسیاب marks the recipient 'to Afrasiab'. Word order places the noun + verb before the source and goal phrases.
segments.s171-g4: که introduces a reported clause 'that ...'. بیدار is the adjective 'awake' qualifying بخت 'fortune/fate'. اندرآمد is here the verb 'entered' (but the provided senses list gives only nominal senses, so the verb sense is not listed), and به خواب 'into sleep' is the prepositional complement: literally 'that wakeful fortune entered into sleep' (i.e. 'fortune fell asleep').
segments.s171-g5: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the very', followed by سرخه (here a personal name or epithet) and the adjective نامور 'famed/renowned'. کشته شد is the passive/perfect construction 'was killed' (کشته past participle + شد auxiliary). The order is demonstrative + name + epithet + passive verb.
segments.s171-g6: چنان 'thus/so' introduces the result clause. دولت here refers to the realm's prosperity/sovereignty, تیز is functioning adverbially as 'swift(ly)', and برگشته شد is again the participle + شد construction meaning 'was turned back/overturned' (i.e. fortunes were quickly reversed). The adjective/adverb (تیز) modifies the manner of برگشته شد.
segments.s171-g7: بریده is a past participle 'cut off' placed before the noun it modifies. سرش 'his head' combines سر + enclitic ش 'his', followed by the object marker را. نگونسار کرد is 'made (it) نگونسار' — here نگونسار is an adjective meaning mournful/woeful or pitiable and کرد is the verb 'did/made', so the structure is 'he made/displayed his severed head mournfully.'
segments.s171-g8: تنش 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش), plus را marks it as the definite object. به خون غرقه is a prepositional phrase 'drenched in blood' (به + noun + غرقه 'drenched'). بر دار کرد is a verbal phrase 'placed on the dar/gallows' (بر 'on' + دار 'gallows/tree' + کرد 'did/put'), meaning 'he hung it on the gallows.'
segments.s171-g81: بدو گفت is the sequence ‘to him (بدو) he said (گفت)’ with the indirect object placed before the verb (common in older poetry). کای is a vocative ‘O’. نام بردار is a compound: نام (name/fame) + بردار (bearer) where بردار is a noun ‘bearer’ (not the imperative), so ‘name-bearer’ = ‘renowned one’; شیر is the vocative noun ‘lion’. The phrase addresses someone poetically as “O renowned lion.”
segments.s171-g82: همانا که is a strengthened connector ‘verily / behold that’ introducing the following clause. پیلت is the elephant (here with possessive sense ‘your elephant’), نیارد is an archaic/present-form verb meaning ‘does not bring / will not cause to come’ (sense list given did not include this verbal meaning, so marked -1), and به زیر is a prepositional phrase ‘under / beneath’ — together: ‘your elephant will not be brought under (you).’
segments.s171-g83: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’. پیلتن here is a proper name/place (given as a place-name in the lexical choices) and را is the definite-object marker. به چنگ آوری is a light-verb phrase: به چنگ (lit. ‘to the claw/grasp’) + آوری (2nd/3rd-person present/subjunctive ‘you/one bring/capture’) meaning ‘you capture / seize’. The construction means ‘if you seize Piltan.’
segments.s171-g84: زمانه is used in the sense of fortune/the world rather than merely ‘era’. برآساید is a poetic present/subjunctive form meaning ‘will be appeased / will relent’. از here introduces the cause (‘because of’), and داوری means ‘judgment’ or ‘adjudication’. Together: ‘the world/fortune will be pacified because of judgment.’
segments.s171-g85: به توران is a locative phrase ‘in/unto Turan’. چو introduces a conditional/comparative clause (‘if/when/like’) here best read as ‘if/when’ (‘if there is no one like you’). تو کس نباشد literally ‘you, someone is not’ — کس ‘anyone/person’ and نباشد is the negative form of ‘to be’ in an older/poetic form (‘is not’); word order is inverted (typical of poetry): ‘if there is no one like you.’
segments.s171-g86: This segment lists domains introduced by repeated به ‘in/for’: به گنج (in treasure/wealth), و به تیغ (and in sword = martial prowess), و به تخت (and in throne), و کلاه (and helmet/cap). The repeated به gives a parallel enumeration of honors/possessions; کلاه here is literal ‘hat/helmet’ in the sense of head-gear or symbolic crown.
segments.s171-g87: به گردان سپهر is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into the turning/revolving heavens’ (گردان as ‘revolving’ modifying سپهر ‘sky’). اندرآری is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/optative ‘you bring in / you admit’ (poetic form). سرم ‘my head’ is the object (سر + possessive -م) placed after the verb — older Persian often places possessed nouns after verbs; the whole yields ‘if you bring my head into the revolving sky.’
segments.s171-g88: سپارم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of سپردن ‘to entrust/hand over’: ‘I will entrust’. The sequence ترا (here split as ت + را in the word list) functions as the old-style object ‘to you’ (ت = 2nd-person suffix/pronoun, را = object marker). دختر و کشورم are the things entrusted: ‘my daughter and my kingdom’ (کشور + possessive -م). The clause therefore expresses a promise: ‘I will entrust to you my daughter and my realm.’
segments.s171-g281: به is the preposition 'to/toward' marking direction. لشکرگه 'encampment/army-camp' is the direct object of that prepositional phrase. خویش + ش together form the possessive/reflexive 'their own' (here split in the segmentation: خوی/ش). گشتند is the simple past, 3rd-person plural of گشتن 'to turn/go/return'; the subject (they/the army) is plural. باز is an adverb postposed to the verb meaning 'back/again' (here 'returned back'). The whole phrase means 'they returned to their own camp.'
segments.s171-g282: This is a nominal comment about the army: سپه 'army/host' is the noun head; یکسر is an adverb here meaning 'entirely/altogether' modifying the phrase; از خواسته is a genitival phrase 'from/of desire(s)/what was wished for' (خواسته = wish/that which was sought); بی‌نیاز is an adjectival compound 'without need' = 'free of want/satisfied.' The sequence reads roughly 'the army, wholly, (was) free from desire.' The copula 'was' is omitted, typical in Persian description.
segments.s171-g283: همه دشت 'the whole plain' — همه here = 'whole/entire' modifying دشت 'plain'. پر is the adjective 'full (of)' and takes a following list of items (آهن و سیم و زر) as its complement: آهن 'iron', سیم 'silver', زر 'gold'. The sentence omits an explicit copula, so the structure reads 'the whole plain (was) full of iron, silver, and gold.'
segments.s171-g284: This is a coordinated list of equipment/items that fills the plain: سنان 'spears' is first, followed by repeated coordinating و 'and'. ستام occurs as a noun here (context indicates a weapon or equipment name rather than the lexical senses 'praise' listed in the lemma table), then کلاه 'hat/helmet' (in epic/poetic usage 'کلاه' = helmet), and کمر 'belt/girdle'. The nouns are simply enumerated with و between them.
segments.s171-g113: برآید is a third‑person form used here with a potential/impersonal sense 'may/can be done' (classical Persian allows verb-first order). به دست من is an agent/instrumental phrase 'by my hand' (به + noun); این کارکرد is the object phrase 'this deed / this action' placed after the instrument for poetic emphasis.
segments.s171-g114: به گرد در اختر بد مگرد is an admonition: مگرد is a negative imperative/prohibitive 'do not turn / do not become' (classical poetic usage), به گردِ ... خوانده می‌شود 'into the orbit/circuit of ...'; در اختر بد 'under / in the (influence of the) evil star.' Word order places the locative phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s171-g115: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is simple past 'he heard'. ز + و here form the poetic contraction زو = از او 'from him' (the scanner split it), and این سخن is the object 'this speech'; شهریار is the subject 'the king', placed after the clause for emphasis (common in epic diction).
segments.s171-g116: یکی is an indefinite 'a / a certain', used before the noun. اسپ is the archaic word for 'horse' (asp). شایسته modifies the horse as 'worthy / fit', and کارزار specifies the domain 'for battle' (an adjective phrase 'fit for combat'; classical Persian often omits the ezāfe).
segments.s171-g117: بدو is the dative/clitic 'to him' (به + او), and داد is the past 'gave'. با introduces an accompaniment/instrument 'with'; تیغ is 'sword'. برگستوان is a word whose precise meaning is obscure here (an item of equipment or phrase), so the scanner's sense list does not yield a clear match; grammatically it follows با as a coordinated object.
segments.s171-g118: همان 'the same / that' picks up or repeats the equipment; نیزه 'spear' and درع 'armor' are coordinated by و. خود is an emphatic/reflexive 'himself' (emphatic identity), and گوان is an adverbial/particle 'so / likewise / thus' attaching back to همین—'and himself too.'
segments.s171-g119: بیاراست is a past tense verb 'he adorned / equipped'. آن جنگ را uses آن + the object‑marker را to mark 'that battle' as the definite object. پیلسم is a specialized/obscure noun in the epic (the lexical note marks it as uncertain/obsolete); syntactically it is the thing with which the battle was adorned/equipped.
segments.s171-g120: همی is an adverb 'continually / indeed' common in epic diction. راند is past 'he drove' (caused to move). چون شیر is a simile 'like a lion.' با باد و دم is an instrumental phrase 'with wind and breath' (i.e., swiftly and with force); باد is the literal 'wind' here rather than the optative particle.
segments.s171-g105: The phrase begins with the prepositional phrase به پیران ('to the elders') marking the indirect object / audience. چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus' (چنین + گفت); گفت is the past-tense verb 'he said'. پس is a connective here meaning 'so/then'. پیلسم is an uncertain/archaic token (probably a name or a vocative) placed after the connective and addressed or invoked by the speaker.
segments.s171-g106: کزین is a prepositional/relative marker 'from this / because of this' introducing the reason clause. پهلوان is the noun 'the champion/hero' (subject of the embedded clause). دل ندارد is a predicate: دل 'heart' + ندارد 'does not have' (present/praeterite meaning 'has not'), so 'does not have a heart ...'. دژم is an adjectival predicate modifying دل ('a sullen/gloomy heart'); word order places the adjective after the verbal predicate.
segments.s171-g107: که‌گر is the conditional 'if' (archaic spelling of کِه اگر). من کنم uses the present/subjunctive form کنم after the conditional to express a hypothetical 'if I were to do ...'. جنگ جنگی is a rhetorical doubling: جنگ (war) + جنگی (warlike) intensifies the noun (a great fight). نهنگ is used metaphorically as a massive/whale-like qualifier, strengthening the scale of the battle.
segments.s171-g108: نیارم here functions as a negative subjunctive/optative 'I would not bring/produce' (an archaic contracted form of نیاورم); it negates the following result. به بخت تو is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/through your fortune' (marking agency/credit), and بر شاه ننگ places 'ننگ' (shame) 'upon the king' with بر as the preposition selecting the target of the shame.
segments.s171-g109: به پیش تو is a fronted locative phrase 'in your presence / before you' (به + پیش). با introduces the comitative 'with'; نامور is the adjective 'renowned' modifying the following compound. چار گرد is a terse poetic compound: چار 'four' + گرد 'about/around' — here best read as 'the four who stood around' or 'the four companions/hosts', a fixed/poetic group description.
segments.s171-g110: چه کردم is a rhetorical clause 'what I did' or 'how I acted' (چه as an interrogative/exclamative). تو دیدی is the reporting clause 'you saw'. ز من is the prepositional phrase 'from me', and دست برد is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'to withdraw one's hand / desist / cease an attack' (دست + برد [past of برداشتن]), so the clause says 'you saw that he withdrew his hand from me.'
segments.s171-g111: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'. کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. زورم is the possessed noun 'my strength' (زور + enclitic -م). افزونترست is a comparative copular predicate 'is greater / is more' (افزون + comparative + -ست copula), so the whole line states an emphatic present claim about increased strength.
segments.s171-g112: شکستن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'breaking' functioning as the subject. دل من 'my heart' is the object of that verbal noun. نه is the negation 'not', and اندرخورست (an archaic adjective + copula) means 'is fitting/appropriate'. The clause literally reads 'Breaking my heart is not fitting' (i.e. it is not right to break my heart).
segments.s171-g193: Literal clause order: indefinite noun + noun (یکی باد) = 'a certain/a wind' as subject, then past verb برخاست 'rose/arose'. The prepositional phrase از رزمگاه 'from the battlefield' follows the verb. 'یکی' here functions as an indefinite 'a/certain'.
segments.s171-g194: هوا را is the object marked by the definite object marker را ('the air'). The verb بپوشید is a past action here meaning 'covered' (classical/past form); گرد is a circumpositional adverb meaning 'around' and سپاه is the noun 'host/army' that it scopes over. Together: 'it covered the air around the host.'
segments.s171-g195: دو لشکر is the plural subject 'two armies/hosts.' به هامون is a directional/prepositional phrase 'into/on the plain.' همی is an archaic/poetic particle indicating a continuous or iterative action, and تاختند is the past third-person plural 'they charged/rushed/attacked.'
segments.s171-g196: یک از دیگران is a partitive phrase 'one of the others.' The verb بازنشناختند is a classical compound form related to 'شناختن' (to recognize) and here functions as the past action describing recognition (contextually typically rendered 'did not recognize one another' in epic narrative). The clause therefore says one did not recognize the other(s).
segments.s171-g197: چون شب introduces a simile 'like/as night.' جهان is the subject 'the world,' followed by two stacked adjectives تیره تاریک (both 'dark') for poetic intensification, and شد is the past third-person singular 'became.' The sequence means 'the world became as dark as night.'
segments.s171-g198: تو گفتی is past second-person singular 'you said.' The following clause is finite: به شب روز نزدیک شد literally 'to night, day drew near' — i.e. 'you said that day had drawn near to night.' نزدیک is an adjectival/adverbial predicate 'near/approaching' and شد is the past 'became/drew.'
segments.s171-g199: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said' (poetic particle + verb), and با لشکر افراسیاب literally 'with the army of Afrasiab' functions idiomatically as 'to Afrasiab's army' (i.e., Afrasiab spoke to his troops). Word order places the name after لشکر.
segments.s171-g200: که introduces reported speech 'that'; بیدار بخت is a compound phrase 'wakeful fortune' used idiomatically here, and اندر آمد به خواب literally 'entered into sleep' — an idiom meaning 'fell asleep' (i.e., fate/fortune deserted them). The sequence is a poetic way to say 'fortune went to sleep/was gone.'
segments.s171-g169: یکی is an indefinite subject meaning 'someone' (not the numeral). زد is the simple past (3rd sing.) of زدن 'to strike/drive' and takes a following locative phrase introduced by بر. بر here means 'at/on' and combines with کمرگاه ('small of the back/waist'). اوی is an enclitic/old poetic pronominal form meaning 'his' (modifies کمرگاه). Word order is Subject - Verb - prepositional/location phrase.
segments.s171-g170: ز زین is a prepositional phrase 'from the saddle' (ز = 'from' in poetic/classical Persian). برگرفتش is the past form بر + گرفت with the enclitic -ش 'him', meaning 'he took/raised him up' or 'seized him up from'. به here introduces a manner phrase 'in/into the manner of', and کردار گوی is a figurative simile: کردار (here functioning as 'manner/deed') + گوی 'sphere/ball' = 'like a ball'. Note برگرفتش is a compound verb (بر-گرفت) with the object clitic attached.
segments.s171-g171: همی is a progressive/adverbial particle here meaning 'kept on' or 'still', and تاخت is the past tense 'charged/rushed'. تا here means 'as far as/until' and introduces the destination/extent قلب توران سپاه 'the heart (center) of Turan's army' (نِـونِین/genitive relation expressed by juxtaposition).
segments.s171-g172: بینداختش is the past (بینداخت) with the object clitic -ش 'him', meaning 'he threw/cast him'. خوار is an adjectival/adverbial qualifier meaning 'lowly/despised' (indicating the manner/result of the casting). در + قلبگاه is a locative phrase 'in the heart/center' (قلبگاه = core/center). The verb precedes its object clitic and the manner adjective follows it, typical of classical poetic word order.
segments.s171-g173: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'he said' used as the reporting verb). کاین را is the direct object 'this one' with را as the definite/object marker. به دیبای زرد is a prepositional phrase 'in (a) yellow brocade' specifying how to clothe him; دیبا 'brocade' is the garment, زرد 'yellow' is the color.
segments.s171-g174: بپوشید here is the jussive/imperative/optative form (3rd person) of پوشیدن, used to give the instruction 'clothe/dress (him)'. کز introduces the subordinate clause and here has a causal/sourcelike sense 'from/for which', گرد means 'dust', شد is the past 'became', and لاژورد is 'lapis-lazuli/azure' — together forming 'for from the dust he became lapis' (a poetic causal/result clause).
segments.s171-g175: عنان را is the object phrase 'the reins' marked by the object particle را. بپیچید is the past form (بر + پیچید) meaning 'he twisted/turned' (here 'twisted the reins'). زان is a poetic contraction of ز + آن 'from that', and جایگاه means 'place' — together 'he twisted the reins from that place.'
segments.s171-g176: بیامد is the past 'came' (here 'descended/fell'), and دمان (دمان) is the hem/skirt (subject of the verb; poetic inversion places it after the verb). تا (here 'until/as far as') plus به قلب سپاه gives the extent/location 'as far as the heart/center of the army.' The phrase thus describes the hem descending down to the midst of the host.
segments.s171-g209: بر رستم آمد is a prepositional + verb phrase: بر (toward/to) + object (رستم) + verb آمد (came), so literally 'came toward Rostam.' یکی چاره‌جوی is an appositive noun phrase 'a certain seeker-of-remedy' placed after the verb; Persian permits this SOV/verb-final order in poetry.
segments.s171-g210: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'یکی چاره‌جوی' (ʼwho/thatʼ). امروز 'today' sets the time; از این (written از + ین) means 'from this' — ین is the orthographic/poetic form of این. رزم is 'battle' and شد is the intransitive 'became' (here acting as copula). رنگ و بوی is a fixed poetic collocation meaning 'color and smell' — here idiomatically 'stained/marked by (the battle)'; the verb links the man to that predicate.
segments.s171-g211: همه modifies رزمگه to mean 'the whole/entire battlefield.' شد is the past/inchoative 'became' and چو introduces a simile 'like' (چو دریای خون = 'like a sea of blood'). Word order is standard for poetic Persian: subject + verb + simile phrase.
segments.s171-g212: This is a nominal clause with an implicit/copied verb from the previous clause: درفش سپهدار ایران is a construct: 'the banner (درفش) of the sepahdar of Iran' (سپهدار ایران). نگون is a postpositive adjective meaning 'overthrown/put down' — in context the verb 'was/lay' is understood from the prior شد.
segments.s171-g213: بیامد is the past 'came.' ز قلب سپه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the heart/core of the host,' and پیلتن is a place-name or appositive specifying that host (’at Piltan’). The phrase as a whole gives provenance: 'he came out of the army's center at Piltan.'
segments.s171-g214: پس is a temporal/consecutive particle 'then.' او فرامرز places the pronoun and proper name together for emphasis ('he, Farāmarz'), and با انجمن means 'with (his) assembly/company/retinue.' The verb 'came' is omitted here but is understood from the previous بیامد.
segments.s171-g215: سپردار بسیار در پیش بود is a copular clause: سپردار 'commanders/commander,' بسیار here modifies as 'many' (numerical sense) rather than 'very,' در پیش 'in front/present,' and بود is the past copula 'were/was' — together: 'many commanders were present/in front.'
segments.s171-g216: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that': دلشان 'their hearts' (دل + possessive شان) is the subject; ز رستم 'toward/from Rostam' indicates the object of feeling; بداندیش is an adjective meaning 'ill‑minded/malicious' (i.e. ill-disposed), and بود is the past copula 'were.' The clause explains why the commanders were present: their hearts were hostile to Rostam.
segments.s171-g217: This is a noun phrase listing the people: همه (all/everyone) + خویِش (kin/kinsmen) with the enclitic ش (his) giving 'his kinsmen', then و (and) followed by پیوندِ افراسیاب (the allies/links of Afrasiab). Note the possessive relation: پیوند افراسیاب = 'Afrasiab's allies'. Word order is head-first for 'همه خویش' and then a genitive construction for 'پیوند افراسیاب'.
segments.s171-g218: A parallel nominal phrase describing their dispositions: همه دل = 'every heart' (همه as distributive 'every/all'), پر از = a two-word expression meaning 'full of' (پر + از), followed by کین 'hatred/vengeance'. Then و links to سر پرشتاب where سر = 'head' (literal head) and پرشتاب = 'full of haste/very hurried'. Note classical/poetic concatenation (no explicit copula).
segments.s171-g219: Main clause: تهمتن (Tahmātan, an epithet/name) is the subject, فراوان functions adverbially/quantitatively 'many/much', از ایشان = 'of them', and بکشت is the archaic past form 'he killed' (root کشتن). Word order is subject — quantifier — object phrase — verb (typical older Persian flexibility).
segments.s171-g220: A separate clause: two names joined by و (فرامرز و طوس) form the subject; اندر آمد is a compound verb meaning 'came in/arrived' (اندر = 'into/among', آمد = 'came'); به پشت = 'to/at the rear/behind' where به indicates direction and پشت = 'back/rear'. Together: 'Faramarz and Tūs came up to the rear.'
segments.s171-g221: Temporal subordinate introduced by چو = 'when'; subject is افراسیاب. آن درفش بنفش is the noun phrase 'that purple banner' (آن = 'that' + درفش 'banner' + بنفش 'purple' as postpositive adjective in classical style). The whole chunk = 'when Afrasiab (saw) that purple banner...'.
segments.s171-g222: نگه کرد is the verbal phrase 'fixed/turned (his) gaze' — نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (past of کردن used with a noun to make a verbal phrase, here analogous to نگاه کرد). بر جایگاه درفش is a prepositional/directive phrase: بر = 'on/upon/toward', جایگاه = 'place/position', درفش = 'the banner' (repeated to specify whose place). So: 'he looked toward/on the position of the banner.'
segments.s171-g223: بدانست = 'he knew/realized' (simple past). کان here is the classical complementizer 'that' (introducing the content clause). پیلتن is a place-name (or a locus marked as 'the one at Piltan'), followed by رستم است = 'is Rostam' (copula + name). Word order: verb (بدانست) + complementizer + locative/name phrase + copular clause.
segments.s171-g224: Two linked predications describing Rostam: سرافراز = 'honoured/high‑standing' (adjective/predicative), then وز تخمه نیرم است is a compressed classical phrasing meaning 'and from the stock/offspring of Nīram is' (وز here functions as a connective 'and/from' in older usage, تخمه = 'offspring/seed', نیرم is a name or lineage marker). The final است is the copula. Overall: 'he is honored and of the lineage of Nīram.'
segments.s171-g225: برآشفت is a past/actional verb meaning that the animal 'sprang up' or 'grew agitated' (epic diction for a sudden movement). The short prepositional phrase بر سان جنگی پلنگ is a simile/nominal group: بر + سان = 'in the manner of / like', followed by the adjective جنگی 'warlike' modifying پلنگ 'leopard'. Word order is demonstrative/epic: verb then simile phrase describing how it leapt.
segments.s171-g226: بیفشارد ران is a verbal phrase: بیفشارد (press/squeeze) + ران (thigh/haunch) — a compact way to say it thrust/pressed its flank forward. پیش او شد به جنگ is a clause where شد (past of شدن) functions as a simple past 'came/arrived' and به جنگ = 'into/for battle'; پیش او 'before him' gives the spatial relation.
segments.s171-g227: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The clause is subject–verb: رستم (subject) + درفش سیه را (definite object: the black banner, with را marking the object) + بدید (past 'saw'). The word order is standard older Persian: temporal marker + subject + object + verb.
segments.s171-g228: به کردار is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/character of' (به used adverbially), followed by شیر ژیان where شیر 'lion' is qualified by ژیان (a poetic epithet, 'living' or 'vital'), and بردمید is a strong past verb 'seized/rose' — here idiomatically 'rose up / sprang forward' with lion-like bearing. The whole segment modifies Rostam's action just described.
segments.s171-g229: به جوش آمد is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'became enraged' or 'was in a boil/ferment' (به + noun + آمد). آن نامبردار is the demonstrative plus epithet 'that renowned one' as the subject; گرد here is a short epic/poetic addition attached to the subject phrase (in older epic Persian such particles often accompany titles). Together the segment sets up the subject and his emotional state.
segments.s171-g230: عنان باره تیزتگ را سپرد is a transitive clause: عنان 'reins/bridle' is the direct object concept, followed by باره (here part of the compound describing the act of taking/handling the reins), then the name/epithet تیزتگ (the steed) marked by را, and سپرد is the past verb 'entrusted/handed over'. The verb governs the object phrase 'the reins of Tiztag' — i.e., he handed the reins to/over (of) the horse/holder.
segments.s171-g231: برآویخت is a past/action verb meaning 'rose up' or 'charged'. The following با + سرکش افراسیاب is an instrumental/associative phrase: با 'with' joins the adjective سرکش 'headstrong/rebellious' to the proper name افراسیاب, i.e., 'the headstrong Afrasiab' — the whole phrase narrates that Afrasiab charged.
segments.s171-g232: به پیگار خون رفت is an idiomatic locative/mission phrase: به + پیگار 'into the battle' + خون رفت 'blood went/flowed' (رفت here = past of رفتن 'to go', used for 'flowed' with blood). چون رود آب is a simile 'like a river of water' (چون = 'like/as', رود = 'river', آب = 'water'), completing the image: blood streamed like a river.
segments.s171-g201: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). سستی is the nominal object meaning 'weakness/slackness' placed before the verb in poetic word order. آرید is an archaic/poetic present/subjunctive or imperative form of آوردن ('bring/show') meaning 'you show' or 'you become', and the following phrase یک تن به جنگ is the affected participant ('one person for the fight') that will be negated in the result clause.
segments.s171-g202: نماند is the negated past/present-resultive form meaning 'will not remain' (ن- + ماند). The sequence م + را here represents the enclitic pronoun + object marker (mـ + را = مرا, 'to/for me') split in the edition; روزگار درنگ is a noun phrase 'fortune/time (روزگار) [a] pause/delay (درنگ)' that is the thing not left to the speaker. Word order (verb before indirect object then object) reflects older/poetic syntax.
segments.s171-g203: آورید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'bring/set up'. بریشان is the fused phrase 'بر ایشان' ('against them') placed before the adverbial ز هر سو ('from every side'); کمین is the noun 'ambush'. The whole segment is an imperative order: 'place ambushes against them from every side.'
segments.s171-g204: به is the preposition here meaning 'with/onto/into' (introducing the manner/result). نیزه 'spear' and خور is the present-stem/verb 'eat' used compositionally (نیزه‌خور = 'spear-eaten' / 'pierced by spears'). اندر زمین 'into the earth' marks the place/result. آورید again is imperative 'bring' or 'cause' — together: 'impale them with spears and throw/drive them into the ground.'
segments.s171-g205: بیامد is simple past 'came'. خود is emphatic 'itself' placed after the verb for emphasis. از قلب توران is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the heart/core of Turan' and سپاه is the noun 'army' which, by poetic inversion, appears after the adverbial phrase rather than before the verb (V S order common in epic poetry).
segments.s171-g206: بر here is the preposition 'upon/against' with طوس the proper name (Tus) as its object. شد is the copular/past 'became' and introduces the resulting state. داغ دل is a genitive/compound 'the burning/scar on the heart' (داغ modifying دل), and کینه‌خواه is a postpositive adjective 'vengeful' or 'revenge-seeking' describing that grief: 'a vengeful grief burned upon Tus's heart.'
segments.s171-g207: This clause front-loads the oblique/genitive phrase از ایران ('from Iran') and the quantifier فراوان ('many') before the patient سپه را (سپھ with the object marker را) — 'many of Iran's army' — followed by the verb بکشت, an archaic past-form meaning 'he slew' (بکشت = 'he killed/slew' in older epic diction).
segments.s171-g208: غمی is an indefinite nominative 'a grief' or 'grief', شد is 'became'/'befell', and دل طوس is the possessive construction 'the heart of Tus' (دل followed by the name). و joins the next verb بنمود ('showed/revealed'), and پشت 'back' is the direct object of بنمود in a formulaic phrase meaning 'he turned his back' — an idiom for retreat or withdrawal.
segments.s171-g161: This clause has two short predications joined by و. گران شد is a literal 'became heavy' (گران + شد, past-tense/resultative), where شد is the auxiliary/verb 'became'. رکیب is the noun subject; here it functions as the one who is affected (the rider/companion). سبک شد عنان is parallel: سبک 'light' + شد 'became', with عنان 'reins' as the subject of that second predication. The two clauses are juxtaposed to show two simultaneous changes.
segments.s171-g162: This is a prepositional/result clause: به چشم 'to/into the eye' (به + چشم) + اندر آورد 'brought in/put into' (آورد as transitive 'bring/put') + رخشان سنان a nominal phrase 'the bright/shining spear' (رخشان modifying سنان). Word order follows classical Persian: locative/goal (به چشم) + verb (آورد) + object (رخشان سنان).
segments.s171-g163: غمی گشت is a simple nominal + verb 'became/wascome' construction: a state 'grief arose' (غمی = grief, گشت = 'became/arose'). The second part, بر لب برآورد کف, is a verbal phrase where the locative modifier بر لب 'onto the lip/edge' precedes the verb برآورد (here 'raised/put up') and its object کف 'palm'. In classical order the prepositional phrase comes before the verb and the object follows the verb.
segments.s171-g164: همی تاخت is a progressive/continuous narrative ('he was rushing' or 'he rushed repeatedly'), with همی functioning as an older continuous marker before the past verb تاخت. از قلب تا پیش صف is a from–to spatial phrase: از 'from' + قلب 'the heart' then تا 'as far as/to' + پیش صف 'before the line/front of the formation'. The whole clause describes motion from the heart toward the front rank.
segments.s171-g165: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said' (چنین adverb 'thus/so' + گفت 'said'). کای is a vocative marker 'O', followed by the epithet نامور 'renowned' modifying the proper name پیلسم. The sequence places the vocative and epithet before the name in the classical poetic order.
segments.s171-g166: مرا خواستی is 'you sought/desired me' (مرا = object 'me', خواستی = 2nd-person past 'you wished/desired/sought'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here (تا + subjunctive): بسوزی is a subjunctive form 'burn' and is understood as the intended/resulting action; به دم is an adverbial 'in a breath/in a moment'. Poetically the subject reference in the subordinate clause can be compressed, but grammatically this is a 'you wanted (me) so that (I) would burn' or more loosely 'you wanted me to burn in an instant.'
segments.s171-g167: همی گفت 'he kept saying' (همی for continuous aspect + گفت 'said') joined by و to می‌تاخت 'he was charging/rushing' (presentive/continuous form). برسان گرد is a short verbal cluster: برسان 'bring/bring near' (here causative/intentional nuance) and گرد (imperative/archaic verbal particle 'turn/become' or a verbalizer) — together indicating 'bringing/coming near' or 'approaching'. The clause therefore mixes narration ('he said') with action ('and he rushed up/approached').
segments.s171-g168: یکی کرد is 'one did/made' but in context functions as an indefinite subject 'someone did/spoke'. کرد is the simple past of کردن 'to do/make' used here to mean 'spoke' when combined with سخن. با او 'with him' marks the addressee; سخن is the noun 'speech/words' (object), and در نبرد 'in the battle' is the locative phrase showing the setting. The normal Persian order subject–predicate–object is followed, with the locative adjunct at the end.
segments.s171-g185: زمین شد is a simple past/resultative copular clause: 'the earth became.' ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing the cause; نعل ستوران is a genitive-like phrase 'the hoofs of the steeds' (hooter + herd/steeds), and ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary.' The verb شد places the resultative state after the subject, a common classical word order.
segments.s171-g186: همه کوه دریا شد is 'all the mountains became sea' with شد as the past copula. The following و دشت کوه omits a repeated شد by ellipsis (poetic economy) and reads 'and the plain (became) a mountain' — دشت is subject, کوه is the predicate noun; the copula is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s171-g187: ز بس is an idiomatic causal phrase 'because of so much' (ز + بس). نعره و ناله is a coordinated noun phrase 'shout and moan,' and کره‌نای qualifies them as the sound of the small reed‑pipe (instrument). The whole phrase names the overwhelming noise causing the following effect.
segments.s171-g188: همی here is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/just' intensifying the statement). آسمان اندر آمد ز جای is a verbal phrase meaning 'the sky moved/was displaced from its place' — اندر آمد is a classical compound ('came in' / 'shifted'), آمد is past tense 'came,' and ز جای 'from its place' marks the origin of motion.
segments.s171-g189: همی is an intensifier ('even/indeed'). سنگ مرجان شد is 'stone became coral' with شد as the past/resultative verb; و خاک خون is elliptical for 'and the earth (became) blood' — the copula شد is omitted the second time, a common poetic device.
segments.s171-g190: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely.' سر سروران is an older-style idafa-like sequence 'the heads of the lords/chiefs' (noun + noun without an explicit ezafe), and شد نگون is the copula plus adjective: 'became overturned/ruined' — نگون here describes a state of being overthrown or fallen.
segments.s171-g191: بکشتند is the past transitive verb 'they killed.' چندان is a quantifier 'so many/much,' and ز هردو گروه is a prepositional phrase 'from/between both groups' specifying the victims' source; word order places the verb before the quantified phrase, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s171-g192: که introduces a result clause 'so that/that' and شد is the past copula 'became.' خاک دریا و هامون چو کوه presents two coordinated results: 'the earth became a sea' and 'the plain (became) like a mountain' — چو is a simile 'like/as,' and the copula is understood for the second conjunct by ellipsis.
segments.s171-g73: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. ایدونک is an older/poetic temporal/particle meaning 'now' or 'at this moment' placed after the condition marker. از من is the prepositional phrase 'from me / toward me' (object of the feeling), and نداری دریغ is a predicative phrase literally 'you have no reluctance' (نداری here functions as 'you do not have' or 'you lack' and دریغ 'reluctance/pity'). The whole segment forms the protasis: 'If now you have no reluctance from me.'
segments.s171-g74: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase 'all at once / in one go' (یکی = 'one', باره = 'occasion/instance' so together 'at one time'). The following و joins a simple noun list: جوشن (armor/ cuirass), گرز (mace), و تیغ (and sword). The verb of giving is omitted (common in poetry); the sequence functions as the apodosis: 'then (give) at once a cuirass, a mace, and a sword.'
segments.s171-g75: ابا is an older verb/noun meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse'; here it appears with the name رستم to indicate lack of reluctance in relation to Rostam (i.e. 'I do not shrink from Rostam' or 'I will not be deterred by Rostam'). امروز 'today' is a temporal adverb, جنگ is the noun 'war/fight', and آورم is first-person singular present/future-like verb 'I bring/produce' (here 'I will wage/bring about war'). The clause thus states the speaker's intent to fight Rostam today.
segments.s171-g76: همه 'all, entirely' modifies نام 'name/reputation' (here 'fame'). او is the third‑person pronoun 'his', and زیر ننگ آورم is a verbal phrase literally 'I bring under shame' (زیر = 'under', ننگ = 'shame', آورم = 'I bring/cause'). Together it means 'I will put all his reputation under disgrace' — the speaker promises to utterly dishonor him.
segments.s171-g77: به پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'before you / in your presence' (به + پیش + تو). آرم is a first-person verb meaning 'I put / I lay / I bring' (classical form of آوردن/آوردن), here functioning as a declarative 'I will present'. سر و رخش او lists the direct objects 'his head and his steed' (سر = head, رخ = horse/steed, with او marking possession). Word order places the locative phrase before the verb, common in classical Persian.
segments.s171-g78: همان خود is a demonstrative/intensive phrase 'that very self / himself' (همان = 'the same/that', خود = intensifier 'self'). و links it to تیغ جهان‌بخش او, where تیغ 'sword' is qualified by the epithet جهان‌بخش 'world-bestowing' (a poetic epithet placed after the noun), and او marks possession. The phrase continues the list of things the speaker will present.
segments.s171-g79: ازو is an ablative/prepositional particle 'because of him / from him' placed initially for emphasis. شاد شد is the compound verb 'became glad / rejoiced' (شاد 'happy' + شد 'became'), and جان افراسیاب 'the soul of Afrasiab' is the subject (جان = soul, with the name as genitive). The word order is inverted for emphasis: the cause (ازو) comes first, then the verb, then the subject.
segments.s171-g80: سر نیزه is a compound 'the tip/head of the spear' (سر = head/tip, نیزه = spear). بگذاشت is the past-tense simple verb 'he placed/put/set' (ب- prefix plus گذاشت). از آفتاب follows as an adverbial/phrase indicating direction or the visual effect with the sun ('toward the sun' / 'in the sun's light'); here it describes how or where the spear's tip was set — in a way that catches the sun. The whole clause narrates Afrasiab's action after rejoicing.
segments.s171-g89: This is a prepositional/temporal phrase: 'از ایران و توران دو' = 'from/of Iran and Turan both' (دو after the two names functions as 'both'). 'بهر آن' = 'for/on account of that' — 'آن' anticipates the predicate in the next segment. 'تست' here is the copula 'thou art' in archaic/poetic Persian placed at the end; the available sense list did not include this verbal sense, so it is marked -1.
segments.s171-g90: 'همان' (= 'that very' / 'the same') modifies the following list of nouns. The nouns 'گوهر و گنج و شهر' are coordinated with و. 'آن' here resumes the demonstrative idea in 'بهر آن' from the previous segment, and the copula 'تست' again (archaic second-person 'thou art') closes the clause, linking the list as the predicate.
segments.s171-g91: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'). The verb 'بشنید' (past 'heard') appears before its subject 'پیران' by poetic inversion: 'when the elders heard'. 'غمی گشت سخت' = 'became severely/grievously sorrowful' — 'گشت' is 'became' and 'سخت' is an intensifier ('severely').
segments.s171-g92: An independent clause: 'بیامد' (past 'came') with the prepositional phrase 'بر شاه' ('upon the king'). 'خورشید بخت' functions as an epithet/appositive ('sun of fortune') — poetic personification meaning that good fortune visited the king.
segments.s171-g93: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him). The content clause starts with the enclitic/relative 'ک' (here written 'کاین' = 'that this'), introducing the reported statement. 'مرد برنا و تیز' is a noun with two adjectival attributes: 'برنا' (youthful/vigorous) and 'تیز' (quick/swift). The lexical entry for 'گفت' in the provided senses was not the verbal past 'said', so it is marked -1.
segments.s171-g94: 'همی' is an adverb of continuity/emphasis ('always/still') placed before the verb. 'بر تن خویش' literally 'upon his body/person' (بر = on/upon, تن = body, خویش+ش = his own), and 'دارد ستیز' = 'he has/keeps strife' i.e. 'he is given to fighting.' Word order (object phrase before verb) is typical of poetic style.
segments.s171-g95: 'همی' again marks habitual/continuous action ('continually'). 'در گمان افتد' = 'falls into imagination/conceit' (در + گمان + افتد), where 'افتد' is the verb 'falls into' and 'از نام خویش' gives the cause or source ('because of his name/reputation') — 'نام' here is best read as 'reputation/fame'.
segments.s171-g96: 'نیندیشد' is negative of 'اندیشیدن' and here means 'does not think/does not consider.' The prepositional phrase 'از کار فرجام خویش' = 'about the outcome of his deeds' (از = concerning, کار = deed/act, فرجام = end/outcome, خویش+ش = his own) shows the object of that lack of thought. The verb is general/presentive (he does not consider).
segments.s171-g129: یکی (an indefinite subject) = 'someone / one (of them)'; نیزه زد is a simple past verb phrase 'struck/ thrust (with a spear)'. گیو را uses را as the definite object marker attached to the name Giv. کز is a fused conjunction (کَه + از) meaning 'from/through which' and introduces the following result/circumstantial clause; نهیب is a loud cry or shock that causes the result.
segments.s171-g130: برون آمدش is a verbal phrase 'came out' where the clitic -ش refers back to Giv ('to him'); هر دو پا = 'both feet' (هر + دو = both); از introduces the source 'from', and رکیب here functions as the thing the feet left (in context: the stirrups/saddle). Word order places the result phrase before the source phrase: 'both feet came out from the rakiib.'
segments.s171-g131: چون here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (listed senses include 'since/because' but in epic use functions as 'when'); the clause is 'چون دید (یار آمدش)' = 'when he saw that a companion had come'. آمدش again carries the clitic -ش 'to him'. Word order places the temporal clause before the main clause.
segments.s171-g132: همی is a frequent epic particle/introductory adverb (here emphatic/continuative, translated 'then/indeed'); یار جنگی is a noun + adjective 'a warlike/valiant companion'; به کار آمدش is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'came to (his) use' = 'came to his aid', with -ش again referring to the beneficiary (him).
segments.s171-g133: یکی 'one (someone)'; تیغ بر نیزه = 'a sword against a spear' where بر means 'against' (striking against); پیلسم is the name attached to نیزه (Pilasm's spear). Word order places instrument and target close together: 'he (with) sword against Pilasm's spear.'
segments.s171-g134: بزد is simple past 'he struck' (subject omitted/understood). نیزه از تیغ او uses از to indicate separation or effect 'the spear, from his sword' (i.e. by contact with his blade); شد is the copular/predicative 'became' and قلم is the resulting state (here effectively 'cut off / rendered useless / blunted' in context; an older/poetic use).
segments.s171-g135: دگر باره is a reduplicated adverbial phrase 'again / once more' (دگر = another, باره = time/occasion). زد is past 'struck'; بر سر = 'upon the head'; ترگ اوی = 'his rag/cloth' where ترگ means a rag or shred and اوی is an archaic possessive/clitic 'his/her', placed after the noun in older poetry.
segments.s171-g136: شکسته شد is a passive/result phrase 'was broken' (شکسته past participle + شد 'became/was'); آن تیغ پرخاشجوی is the noun phrase 'that aggressive / quarrelsome blade', with پرخاشجوی as a descriptive adjective following تیغ and آن the demonstrative 'that'.
segments.s171-g233: This noun phrase is a compressed apposition: 'یکی' (a certain) + 'نیزه‌سالار' (spear‑commander) + 'توران سپاه' (Turan's army/host). Word order is noun‑modifier (one + title + genitive), with 'توران سپاه' functioning as a genitive phrase ('the army of Turan'). This is a typical terse epic nominal group identifying an enemy leader.
segments.s171-g234: 'بزد' is the simple past (perfective) verb 'he struck/attacked'. The doubled preposition 'بر بر' is a poetic emphasis; grammatically it marks the target ('against / at') and is followed by the proper name 'رستم'. 'کینه‌خواه' is an epithet (post‑nominal adjective) 'the avenger/the vengeful one' describing Rostam. The whole clause means 'he struck at Rostam, the Avenger.'
segments.s171-g235: Here 'سنان' is a weapon name (treated as a noun), followed by the locative/goal phrase 'اندر آمد به بند کمر' literally 'entered into the band of the waist/girth.' 'اندر آمد' is a past verbal compound meaning 'came/entered into' and 'بند کمر' = 'girdle / cinch of the waist' (the strap around the body or the horse's girth). The sequence describes a spear's point lodging into the belt/girth.
segments.s171-g236: This clause names a famed weapon, written here as two nouns 'ببر بیان' (Babr‑e‑Bayan). The preposition 'بر' (here 'against') plus the negating/defective verb 'نبد' ('there was not / was of no effect') and adjective 'کارگر' ('effective') form 'بر نبد کارگر' = 'had no effect against it' or 'was not effective.' The line therefore says the named weapon availed nothing in that instance. Note that the constituent is idiomatic/poetic and uses an older word order.
segments.s171-g237: 'تهمتن' is an epithet for Rostam used as the subject. 'به کین اندر آورد روی' is an idiomatic verbal phrase: 'به کین' ('toward vengeance/with vengeance'), 'اندر آورد' (caused/turned, past), 'روی' (face). Together it means 'he turned his face toward vengeance' — i.e., he prepared to take revenge. The verb is past, and the prepositional phrase expresses motive/aim.
segments.s171-g238: This is a simple past clause: 'یکی نیزه زد' ('one spear struck') where 'یکی' is 'a certain/one', 'نیزه' = 'spear' and 'زد' is the past tense 'hit/struck'. The preposition 'بر' marks the target 'سر اسپ اوی' = 'the head of his horse' (سر = head, اسپ = horse, اوی = his). The clause reports the projectile hitting the horse's head.
segments.s171-g239: 'تگاور' is the subject 'rider/warrior.' The preposition 'ز' here means 'from/because of' and 'درد' is 'pain.' 'اندر آمد به سر' is an idiomatic construction: literally 'came into the head' — i.e., the pain came upon his head (he was seized by a headache). 'آمد' is past tense 'came.' This phrase expresses sudden affliction.
segments.s171-g240: 'بیفتاد' is the simple past 'he fell / he fell down.' The clitic 'زو' = 'from him / of him' (older form of 'از او') indicates source or separation. The final noun phrase 'شاه پرخاشخر' is an epithetal nominal ('the quarrelsome/hostile king' or 'the king, quarrelsome one') — here it functions as the entity that fell or as a descriptive tag tied to the falling. The clause is compressed epic diction: 'he fell; the quarrelsome king (fell) from him.'
segments.s171-g265: سپردند is a 3rd-person plural past verb ('they put/placed/handed'); here it governs the following objects. اسپان (horses) appears after the verb (poetic word order: V + object). همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle, coloring the action as ongoing or emphatic. خون به نعل is a directional/complement phrase ('blood onto the horseshoe') where به marks the target (onto/on).
segments.s171-g266: شده is a past participle/auxiliary meaning 'has become' or 'became' placed before the nominal phrase (poetic inversion). پای پیل ('the elephant's foot') is the subject/patient; از introduces the source ('from/out of') and links to دل کشته ('the heart of the slain'), a genitive construction where کشته qualifies the person. لعل is the predicative noun ('ruby' / 'ruby-red'), placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s171-g267: هزیمت (rout/defeat) is the noun head; گرفتند is a 3rd-person plural past verb ('they took/received') used idiomatically with هزیمت to mean 'were routed' or 'suffered a rout'. ترکان (the Turks) is the logical subject/agent placed after the verb (inverted poetic order). چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' modifying the manner of the rout.
segments.s171-g268: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that/as'). رستم is the subject. ز is the poetic variant of از ('from') and takes بازو ('arm' or 'strength') as its object; here بازو can be literal or metonymic for force. همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle. داد داد repeats the verb داد ('gave') for iterative/emphatic effect — classical Persian often repeats a verb to mean 'gave repeatedly' or 'kept giving'.
segments.s171-g269: سه فرسنگ is a distance expression ('three parasangs/farsangs') placed up front. چون is a simile marker ('like/as'). اژدهای دمان is a poetic compound: اژدهای ('dragon') modified by دمان (the 'skirt' or 'foothill/edge'), so 'a dragon of the foothills' — a vivid simile describing appearance or aspect at that distance.
