segments.s171-g270: تهمتن (Tahmātan, an epithet of Rostam) is the subject. همی شد is the classical/perfective phrasing for 'became/grew' (همی adding habitual/emphatic shade, شد = 'became'). پس ('then') places the result in sequence. بدگمان is the predicate adjective 'suspicious' or 'uneasy', completing the clause.
segments.s171-g271: وز آن جایگه is a prepositional/time/place phrase: آن جایگه = 'that place'. وز here functions poetically as a connective/prepositional form equivalent to 'از'/'و از' ('from' / 'and from'), introducing the origin. پیلتن is a proper name/place and بازگشت is the verb 'returned' (here used as a verb, past tense), so the clause means 'from that place Piltan returned.'
segments.s171-g272: سپه is 'the army' (subject). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether' modifying the following predicate. از جنگ ناساز is a prepositional phrase where از (here 'with regard to' / 'in respect of') links جنگ ('war') to ناساز ('unsuited/unfit'); together they mean 'unsuited for war.' گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became' ('turned into' / 'was made'), so the whole clause says the army became wholly unfit for battle.
segments.s171-g145: و is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this clause to the previous thought; دیگر here has an adverbial sense 'moreover/besides' (an additive), and که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'/'who'). از + نامور بخردان is a prepositional phrase 'from the renowned wise men' where نامور (prepositive) modifies بخردان (an archaic plural noun 'wise ones'). Word order is the expected Persian preposition + noun phrase.
segments.s171-g146: ز (poetic form of از) means 'from'; گفت is a noun here meaning 'saying/words' or 'speech'; ستاره‌شمر موبدان is a genitive phrase 'the star‑counter/astrologer of the mobeds' (موبدان = the mobeds, priests). So the whole segment reads literally 'from the words of the mobeds' star‑reader.'
segments.s171-g147: ز = 'from'; اختر 'star' is the agent/source ('from the stars'); بد و نیک are coordinate adjectives 'bad and good' (here 'ill‑omened and auspicious'); بشنوده بود is a compound past: بشنوده (perfect participle 'having heard') + بود (past auxiliary) → 'had heard' (pluperfect/past perfect sense).
segments.s171-g148: جهان را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the world' as the domain); چپ و راست 'left and right' is an idiomatic pairing meaning 'in every direction' or 'on all sides'; پیموده بود is again participle + auxiliary ('had traversed'). Word order is topic/object (جهان را) followed by the locational/adverbial phrase and then the verbal compound.
segments.s171-g149: که‌گر is an archaic contraction meaning 'if' (conditional). پیلسم is a proper name or an obsolete lexical item (here treated as a personal name). از + بد روزگار is a prepositional phrase 'because of ill fortune' or 'through bad times' where بد modifies روزگار (here 'fate/fortune'). The clause sets up a conditional: 'If Pilasm, by ill fortune...'.
segments.s171-g150: خرد is the noun 'wisdom/intellect' serving as the object of یابد. یابد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of یافتن 'to find/obtain' used here in a conditional sense ('should obtain' / 'finds'). و is the coordinating 'and'. بند آموزگار is a compound noun phrase: بند 'bond/fetter' + آموزگار 'teacher' → literally 'teacher of bonds' (i.e. one who instructs about bonds/restraint); this is literal wording and may be idiomatic in context.
segments.s171-g151: نبرده is a negative perfect construction meaning 'has not taken/achieved' (here 'had not accomplished'); چنو (variant of چنان) means 'such' or 'so'; در جهان سر به سر is an adverbial phrase meaning 'throughout the world' or 'everywhere' (سر به سر emphasizes entirety). Word order places the negated verb first (classical/poetic inversion) followed by the scope ('such') and the domain.
segments.s171-g152: به ایران و توران gives the target/direction ('toward/against Iran and Turan'); نبندد is the negative present/jussive of بستن 'to bind' used idiomatically with کمر 'waist' to mean 'gird one's loins' (prepare for action/war) — here negated: 'would not gird (his) waist'. کمر is literal 'waist/girdle' used metonymically for readiness/strength.
segments.s171-g257: برآمد is a past tense verb placed at the start ('arose/issued forth'). ز هر سو is an ablative prepositional phrase ('from every side/direction') that follows the verb in this poetic word order. دم کرنای is a nominal phrase 'the blast (دم) of the trumpet (کرنای)' where the genitive relation is implied without an explicit ezafe, a common feature in classical Persian verse.
segments.s171-g258: همی is a poetic particle here used as an intensifier/aspect marker before the clause (it often signals ongoing or emphatic action). آسمان is the grammatical subject 'the sky', اندر آمد is a compound verb meaning 'came into / rose' and ز جای is an ablative phrase 'from its place' (ز = from, جای = place). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: particle + subject + verb + ablative.
segments.s171-g259: به ابر functions as a directional prepositional phrase 'toward/into the cloud(s)'. اندر آمد is a compounded verb meaning 'rose up / came into', placed after the prepositional phrase in the verse. خروش سران is a genitive construction 'the roar/cry of the chiefs' (سران = leaders) with the possessor following the possessed noun without an overt ezafe, which is frequent in epic poetry.
segments.s171-g260: گراییدن is an infinitival/verbal noun form 'turning/ inclining' used here as a predicate-like phrase; classical Persian often omits an explicit finite verb in such lines, giving a participial or nominal sense. گرزهای is the plural 'maces (گرز) + plural suffix -ها' and گران is the adjective 'heavy' following the noun (Persian adjectives normally follow the noun). Together they mean 'the heavy maces (turning/inclining)'.
segments.s171-g261: گوان is a simile/particle 'like/thus'. سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'one by one' or 'one and all/throughout'; here it modifies the verb that follows. نعره برداشتند is a past plural verb phrase 'they raised/uttered a shout' (برداشتند 'they took up/raised'), with برداشتند as the finite past verb agreeing with an implied plural subject ('they').
segments.s171-g262: سنانها is the plural 'the spears' (سنان + plural -ها). به ابر اندر is a compound directional phrase 'toward/into the clouds' (به + ابر + اندر creating the sense 'into the cloud(s)'), and افراشتند is the past plural verb 'they raised' or 'they lifted up'. The verb agrees with the plural noun سنانها; again the genitive/possession and prepositional relations are typical of epic diction.
segments.s171-g263: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'. سر به سر here is an adverbial idiom 'all over / everywhere'. کشته و خسته are two coordinating adjectives 'slain (killed) and exhausted/weary' joined by و, and بود is the past copula 'was' agreeing with the subject. The whole predicate (سر به سر کشته و خسته بود) describes the state of the earth in the past.
segments.s171-g264: وگر is a conditional/contrastive particle 'and if / and as if'. لاله is 'tulip' (here image of red flowers), بر is the preposition 'on/upon', زعفران is 'saffron' (literally saffron or saffron-colored ground), and رسته بود is the past perfect-like construction 'had grown/sprung up' (رسته = grown, بود = was/had). The clause is a hypothetical or vivid metaphor ('as if tulips had grown on the saffron').
segments.s171-g97: This clause is a negated statement: 'کسی' (no one) is the logical subject, 'سوی دوزخ' gives direction ('toward Hell'), and 'نپوید به پا' is a poetic negative verb phrase meaning 'does not set foot' or 'does not venture (to go)'. Word order is subject — direction — verb phrase; 'به پا' literally 'with foot' = 'set foot'.
segments.s171-g98: 'وگر' = 'and if' introduces a hypothetical continuation. 'خیره' is a predicative/adverbial adjective meaning 'gazing/staring', 'سوی دم اژدها' gives the direction/object of that gaze: 'toward the breath of the dragon'. The clause is left incomplete here and connects to what follows.
segments.s171-g99: 'گر' introduces a condition ('if'). 'او' is the 3rd-person subject 'he'. 'با تهمتن' means 'with Tahmētan' (i.e., against Tahmētan). 'نبرد آورد' is a literary/periphrastic verbal phrase meaning 'would bring/engage in battle' or simply 'fought' — here it forms the protasis of the conditional.
segments.s171-g100: 'سر خویش' = 'his own head' (here 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' together function as 'خویش' = 'own / himself'); 'را' is the definite-object marker. 'زیر' = 'under/beneath' and 'گرد آورد' (literally 'bring/put') is a poetic verb phrase meaning 'to make go beneath' — i.e., to lower or bow. Together the phrase means he would make his own head go down / bow (be humbled).
segments.s171-g101: شکسته شود is a passive participle + present/subjunctive auxiliary construction meaning 'be broken' or 'should be broken' (possibility/conditional). دل گوان را places the patient (the brave's heart) before the adverbial with را marking it as definite/object. به جنگ is an adverbial phrase 'in battle/into war' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s171-g102: بود is the copula (here past/conditional sense 'would be' / 'was') placed before the subject for poetic word order. این سخن نیز is the topical subject 'this saying, too,' and بر شاه ننگ is a prepositional predicate meaning 'a disgrace upon/for the king' (بر marks the relation 'upon/for').
segments.s171-g103: برادر is a vocative 'brother' addressing someone; تو دانی is 'you know' with verb after the pronoun. که introduces a subordinate clause, and کهتر بود is the predicate of that clause 'was the lesser/inferior.'
segments.s171-g104: فزون‌تر is a comparative/adverbial 'more/much greater' modifying the predicate; مهر مهتر بود is the main clause 'the elder's affection was (greater).' برو here is an archaic/poetic particle whose literal 'go' sense does not fit the context and functions as an intensifier or connective in poetic register.
segments.s171-g177: ببارید is a past verb (classical/poetic past) meaning ‘fell’ or ‘shed’ (here of drops); in Persian poetry the verb can precede its subject. پیران is the subject 'the elders'. ز مژگان سرشک is a genitive/complimentary phrase 'teardrops from (their) eyelashes' (ز = 'from', مژگان = 'eyelashes', سرشک = 'teardrop').
segments.s171-g178: تن پیلسم is an idāfa-like phrase 'the body of Pīl-sam' (تن = body, پیلسم a proper/name-like term here). دور modifies the verb as an adverb 'from afar / at a distance'. دید is past 'saw / was seen'. از پزشک here marks the agent (common in classical Persian: agent follows the verb with از), so the clause reads 'was seen from afar by the physician.'
segments.s171-g179: دل governs the following nouns: دل لشکر = 'the heart(s) of the host/army'. و connects coordinate elements; شاه توران سپاه is a compact poetic grouping referring to 'the king of Turan and his host' or 'the Turanian king's army' — poets often stack modifiers without extra particles, so دل scopes over the coordinated phrases.
segments.s171-g180: شکسته شد is a passive/periphrastic past 'was broken' (شکسته = 'broken', شد = auxiliary 'became/was'). و links the next predicate. تیره شد is another past-clause 'became dark/ominous', with رزمگاه ('battlefield') as its noun; the second predicate describes the state of the battlefield.
segments.s171-g181: خروش آمد is a verbal-noun + past verb construction meaning 'a cry/roar arose' (خروش = 'roar, cry', آمد = 'came/was heard'). از لشکر marks the source 'from the army'. هر دو سوی is an adverbial phrase 'on both sides / from both directions' (هر دو = 'both', سوی = 'side').
segments.s171-g182: ده و دار are two coordinated nouns 'village and dwelling/abode' (common poetic pairing for settlements/camps). گردان here functions as a noun 'troop/unit' and links genitively to the settlements (the villages/dwellings of the troop). پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive/quarrelsome' describing that troop (گردانِ پرخاشجوی).
segments.s171-g183: خروشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'the roaring/sounding' used as a noun here; کوس is the object 'drum/signal-drum' (so 'the sounding of the drum'). بر پشت پیل is a locative phrase 'on the elephant's back' (بر = on, پشت = back, پیل = elephant).
segments.s171-g184: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = every side). همی رفت is an iterative/imperfective past 'kept going / spread forth' (همی marks continuity/habit). تا چند میل means 'for/as far as several miles' — تا here = 'as far as', چند = 'several'. (Note: میل here denotes distance 'mile' in poetic usage; that specific sense is not in the provided sense list.)
segments.s171-g153: همانا is an emphatic adverb meaning “verily/indeed.” که introduces the content clause that follows. او را زمان آمدست is an inverted, somewhat archaic construction: زمان (the ‘time’ or moment) is the grammatical subject and آمدست (an archaic perfect/auxiliary ‘has come’) is the verb; او را is marked by the object/beneficiary marker را to indicate the person for whom the time has arrived (a dative‑like use).
segments.s171-g154: که here continues the subordinate clause (‘that’ / ‘for’). ایدر = ‘here’ (adverb). به + چنگم ... آمدست: به takes the sense ‘into/toward’; چنگم is the stem چنگ (grasp/seize) with the 1st‑person possessive -م = ‘my grasp’ (poetic nominalization of the verb stem); دمان (دامن) = ‘hem/skirt’. آمدست is the same archaic perfect ‘has come’. The clause means literally ‘that here into my grasp his hem has come.’
segments.s171-g155: به لشکر بفرمود is an indirect‑speech/imperative construction: به لشکر = ‘to the host/army’, بفرمود is the honorific/simple past ‘he ordered/said’. کز is the fused form of که + از meaning here ‘from/that from’; جای خویش literally ‘their own place’ (جای = place; خویش is reflexive/possessive formed here from خوی + the enclitic ش = ‘their’).
segments.s171-g156: مگر introduces the permitted exception (‘except/only that’). ناورند is a negative jussive/optative form meaning roughly ‘they do not move/advance’ in this context (the corpus gloss gives ‘they do not bring’ but here it functions as ‘do not depart’). اندکی = ‘a little’; پای پیش = an idiom ‘to put the foot forward’ (پای = foot, پیش = forward/before). Together: ‘except that they may put a little foot forward.’
segments.s171-g157: شوم is a 1st‑person subjunctive/jussive form meaning ‘may I become’ or idiomatically ‘let me’ (here a formulaic wish/resolve). برگرایم is an archaic 1st‑person subjunctive/indicative of برگشتن/برگردانیدن meaning ‘I turn/return’ (the form -ایم marks 1st person). تن = ‘body’. پیلسم is an archaic/uncertain lexeme (lemma marked ‘obsolete/uncertain’); grammatically it is a noun attached to تن (a kind of body or an animal‑body reference in context).
segments.s171-g158: ببینم is 1st‑person subjunctive ‘let me see’ / ‘I will see’. که introduces an indirect question ‘whether/if’. دارد is the present auxiliary/lexical verb ‘has/possesses’. پی here means ‘foot’ (or track/footing) in the concrete sense; شاخ = ‘horn’; دم in this context denotes the animal’s tail/tip (the lemma list gives ‘tip/edge’ as the closest sense). The clause is a straightforward present possession inquiry: ‘I will see whether it has feet and horns and a tail.’
segments.s171-g159: یکی = ‘one’ or ‘a certain one’. نیزه = ‘spear’. بارکش here functions as an agentive/compound element meaning ‘bearer’ (literally ‘load‑carrier’), so together نیزه بارکش = ‘spear‑bearer’ (a person who carries a spear). برگرفت is the past tense ‘took up/raised’. The sequence is a simple past narrative: ‘one spear‑bearer took up (his) spear.’
segments.s171-g160: بیفشارد is an archaic/poetic verb form (lemma given as ‘press/squeeze’); grammatically it is a past tense/action verb. ران = ‘thigh’; ترگ = ‘rag/shred’. بر + سر + گرفت is an idiomatic sequence: بر = ‘on/upon’, سر = ‘head’, گرفت = ‘took/seized’ — together ‘took (it) on (his) head’ or ‘placed it on his head.’ So the clause describes pressing a rag to the thigh and putting it on the head.
segments.s171-g273: This phrase literally orders as 'from Rostam asked Tūs' but Persian word order here places the prepositional phrase 'ز رستم' (from Rostam) before the verb 'بپرسید' (asked) and the epithet 'پرمایه طوس' (the substantial/seasoned Tūs). In natural English: 'Tūs the seasoned asked of Rostam...'.
segments.s171-g274: This is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' (that/which) and 'چون' here means 'how' (in what manner). 'یافت' is the past 3rd‑person 'found/obtained' and 'شیر' is 'lion'. 'از یکی گور کوس' is literal 'from a certain grave (گور) [and] drum (کوس)', a compressed poetic phrasing describing the source/location — rendered here as 'from a certain grave'.
segments.s171-g275: 'بدو' is the enclitic/prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; Persian often places the indirect object before the verb. 'گفت' here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (note: the supplied sense list did not include 'said', so that sense is marked below as -1). 'رستم' is the subject name; 'که' introduces the following subordinate clause; 'گرز گران' is a noun phrase 'heavy mace'.
segments.s171-g276: 'چو' here means 'when'. 'یاد آرد' is a verbal phrase: 'یاد' (reminder/memory) + the verb 'آرد' (brings) — together 'brings to mind / evokes'. 'از یال جنگ‌آوران' gives the thing evoked: 'of the mane/crest of the warriors.' The clause describes the condition under which what follows happens.
segments.s171-g277: A terse nominal phrase: 'دل سنگ و سندان' lists two metaphoric hard things ('heart of stone' and 'anvil'). 'نماند' is past third‑person negative 'did not remain / was not left', and 'درست' here means 'intact / whole.' Together: 'No heart of stone nor anvil remained intact.'
segments.s171-g278: 'بر و یال' is a coordinated object phrase; 'بر' in this context functions as 'against / the forehead' (a target of striking) and 'یال' is 'mane/crest'. 'کوبنده' is the agentive/participial adjective 'striking' (i.e. the striker), and 'باید نخست' is modal + adverbial: 'must first.' The whole clause prescribes the action: 'the striker must first strike the forehead and mane.'
segments.s171-g279: 'عمودی' is a noun meaning 'an upright / a vertical post'. The relative clause 'که کوبنده هومان بود' follows: 'که' = 'which/that', 'کوبنده' = 'the striker' or 'striking one', 'هومان' is the proper name, and 'بود' = past 'was'. The clause describes the post by saying that Homan was the striker associated with it (i.e. Homan was the one who wielded/struck it).
segments.s171-g280: This sentence contrasts outer appearance and inner substance. 'تو' here is an emphatic/pointing particle (classical/poetic) introducing the comment; 'آهن' = 'iron' names the outer material; 'مخوانش' in context means its outward covering/appearance (the supplied dictionary senses do not match this use, see note). 'که' = 'that', 'موم' = 'wax', 'آن' = 'that/its', and 'بود' = 'was'. Literally: 'Its outside was iron, that its inside was wax.'
segments.s171-g241: همی + past-verb is the epic/progressive iterative marker: همی جست = '(he) leapt (again/at once)'. The verb جست (past of جهیدن) appears before the subject رستم (typical poetic V–S order). کمرگاه او is the target/place phrase 'his flank/loin' with the possessive pronoun او placed after the noun.
segments.s171-g242: که here introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that, in order that'). از رزم literally 'from the battle' = 'in/for the fight'. کوته کند is 'makes short' (کوته as adjective/verb stem + کند, classical present/optative form meaning 'causes to be short' = 'shorten(s)'). راه او = 'his way/route' (possessive after noun).
segments.s171-g243: نگه (a noun 'gaze/glance') + کرد ('made/did') forms the verbal phrase 'cast a look' (نگاه کردن pattern). The subject هومان follows because of poetic word order; بدید is simple past 'saw'. از کران 'from the edge/afar' indicates distance/source of his sight.
segments.s171-g244: به marks direction/toward (here 'toward the neck'); گردن is 'neck'; برآورد is a past tense verb 'he raised/brought up' (classical compound برآوردن used here as 'lifted up'); گرز گران is noun + adjective in poetic order = 'heavy mace'.
segments.s171-g245: بزد is the simple past 'he struck/struck at'. The prepositional phrase بر سر شانه پیلتن = 'upon the head/top of Piltan's shoulder' (بر = on/upon, سر شانه = top of the shoulder, پیلتن names the person or place associated). Word order places the verb before the location phrase.
segments.s171-g246: به لشکر marks the target/recipient 'to the army/host'. خروش آمد is an impersonal/nominal phrase 'a cry/roar came' (arrived). از انجمن gives the source 'from the assembly'. The construction literally: 'To the army a cry came from the assembly.'
segments.s171-g247: ز پس (poetic) = 'after that/thereupon'. کرد + نگاه = 'made/turned a glance' (نگاه کردن pattern), with رستم as the subject placed after کرد (poetic inversion). همانگه is an adverb 'immediately/at that moment'.
segments.s171-g248: بجست is past 'leapt/sprung' (here the leaping sense). از marks origin 'from'; کفش literally 'shoe' (here a physical point of departure); نامبردار is an adjective 'renowned/aforementioned' modifying شاه 'king' — the sequence 'کفش نامبردار شاه' reads 'the shoe of the renowned king'.
segments.s171-g121: به ایرانیان is an indirect-object prepositional phrase ('to the Iranians'). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/asked' (3rd sg.). رستم is the grammatical subject placed after the verb (permissible poetic inversion). کجاست is a fused question phrase ('where is...') functioning as direct speech/content of گفت.
segments.s171-g122: The initial که introduces a relative/subordinate clause linked to the question 'کجاست'. گوید is a present/historic-present reporting verb here meaning 'is said' / 'says' (used in narration to introduce reported content). The second که introduces the reported clause 'that he is ...'. او is the pronoun 'he', روز جنگ is a genitival phrase 'day of battle', and اژدهاست is a copular predicate 'is a dragon' (compound 'اژدها + است').
segments.s171-g123: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بشنید is past tense 3rd singular 'he heard' (چو + past = 'when he heard'). گیو is the subject that follows the verb (typical flexible word order). این سخن is the object 'this speech / these words'. بردمید is a past verb meaning roughly 'seized / sprang up / took hold' — here it indicates an immediate physical reaction ('he leapt up / sprang to action').
segments.s171-g124: This is two coordinated actions: بزد is past 'he struck' (3rd sg.) with the object دست ('with his hand' — دست here marks the striking instrument/body-part). و simply connects the next action. تیغ is the object 'sword'; از میان is a prepositional phrase 'from among / out of', and برکشید is past 'he drew up/pulled out' (here 'drew his sword out'). The sequence describes: he struck (with his hand) and drew his sword forth.
segments.s171-g125: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object). گفت is past 'said'; رستم is the subject. به یک ترک جنگ is a prepositional + nominal phrase ('to/against one Turk war') where به + opponent + جنگ (the noun 'war/fight') expresses the act of making war on a single Turk. Word order places the speaker and the admonition compactly in poetic style.
segments.s171-g126: نسازد is a negative form meaning 'it does not become / it is not fitting' (literally from ساختن but here idiomatic 'befit'); همانا is an emphatic adverb 'indeed/truly'. که introduces the result/clause that follows: آیدش = 'will come to him' (آید + enclitic -ش 'to him'), and ننگ = 'shame'. Together: 'It is not fitting ... for surely shame will come upon him.'
segments.s171-g127: برآویختند is a 3rd-plural past verb 'they leapt upon / sprang at' (compound/poetic verb). آن دو جنگی is a nominal phrase 'those two warriors/fighters' (جنگی as a noun/adjective meaning 'warlike person' or 'fighter'). به هم is a reciprocal/adverbial phrase 'at one another / upon each other'. The clause describes a mutual attack between the two fighters.
segments.s171-g128: This short sequence names the persons involved. دمان here introduces or brackets the following names (literally 'hem/skirt' in the lexicon but used poetically as a locator/brief pause); گیو and گودرز are proper names (Giv and Goudarz). با is the preposition 'with', linking پیلسم (a personal name) as accompanying/associated with them. In context this lists who is engaged or present in the clash.
